did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper utilized Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), a deep learning method, to automate the classification of skin diseases, specifically acne and actinic keratosis. The study reports achieving an accuracy of 96.4% (and 97.858% in results discussion) for identifying seven distinct types of skin lesions using a CNN-based model trained on dermatoscopic images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and training of a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model for real-time skin disease identification. It includes specific sections on methodology (Proposed System), experimental results (accuracy of 96.4% and 97.858%), and performance metrics (precision, recall, F1-scores), which are characteristic of a research study rather than a review. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICNSoC), it reports new technical results.

title: DERMA AI: Real-time Skin Disease Identification Using CNNs for Acne and Actinic keratosis
journal: 2025 Second International Conference on Networks and Soft Computing (ICNSoC)
brief_summary: This study proposes a real-time skin disease identification system using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to automate the classification of acne and actinic keratosis. The model was trained on a dataset comprising dermatoscopic images of acne and benign skin cancer lesions, leveraging deep feature extraction for precise lesion detection. The system architecture includes modules for input data acquisition, pre-processing, segmentation, and classification using a CNN with convolutional, pooling, and fully connected layers. Experimental results demonstrated an impressive test accuracy of 97.858% in differentiating between seven distinct types of skin lesions, with balanced performance confirmed by precision, recall, and F1-scores. The findings highlight the model's reliability and potential to serve as a scalable, cost-effective diagnostic tool for dermatologists and patients.

all_task_types.target: Seven distinct types of skin lesions (including acne, actinic keratosis, melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, etc.)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the CNN model is trained for 'automated classification of acne and actinic keratosis' and differentiates 'between seven distinct types of skin lesions'. The system provides a 'confidence score' for class prediction. Performance is evaluated using accuracy (96.4% and 97.858%), precision, recall, and F1-scores, which are standard metrics for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: Custom Dermoscopic Image Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset comprising dermatoscopic images of acne and benign skin cancer lesions, used to train the proposed CNN model for real-time skin disease identification.

use.training: Used to train the CNN model for automated classification of acne and actinic keratosis.
use.validation: Used for validation during model training (loss and accuracy charts reported).
use.testing: Used for testing to evaluate model performance (reported test accuracy of 97.858%).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy (96.4% and 97.858%), precision, recall, and F1-scores.

Custom Dermoscopic Image Dataset.notes: The model differentiated between seven distinct types of skin lesions. The abstract reports an accuracy of 96.4%, while the Results & Discussion section reports a test accuracy of 97.858% and an F1-score of 96.8%. Performance was confirmed by balanced precision and recall across categories. The model architecture included convolutional, pooling, and fully connected layers.

architecture_details.layers: Multiple convolutional, pooling, and fully connected layers

training_details.preprocessing: Noise reduction, resizing, normalization, scaling, and augmentation

classification_or_output_head.classes: Seven distinct types of skin lesions

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Mentioned as a preprocessing technique used to enhance the quality and balance the dataset before inputting raw images into the CNN model. Specific geometric transforms, color transforms, probabilities, or ranges are not reported in the provided text.

conclusions: The proposed CNN model, consisting of convolutional, pooling, and fully connected layers, demonstrated the ability to differentiate between seven distinct types of skin lesions with a test accuracy of 97.858%.
The model achieved balanced performance across categories, confirmed by precision, recall, and an F1-score of 96.8%, indicating it does not produce many false alarms or miss significant cases.
The model showed good generalization capabilities, as indicated by improving training and test F1-scores and closely moving training and validation accuracy lines, suggesting limited overfitting.
The system provides a scalable, cost-effective, and real-time solution for skin disease identification, specifically for acne and actinic keratosis, leveraging deep feature extraction.
The developed system enables automated lesion counting, severity tracking, and treatment progress monitoring, offering a more efficient and accessible diagnostic tool.
AI-driven technologies like CNNs have the potential to transform clinical diagnostic processes by providing a high-throughput, precise, and non-invasive tool that complements dermatologists' skills.
The system is designed to enhance screening and allow for early intervention for skin cancer and other skin disorders, particularly in remote or resource-limited areas.

The study acknowledges limitations regarding the accurate classification of acne and actinic keratosis specifically, the need for real-time processing capabilities, and challenges related to accessibility and usability in resource-limited settings.
important_remarks: The paper acknowledges limitations regarding the accurate classification of acne and actinic keratosis specifically, noting that current AI-driven models are not specific enough for these conditions.
The study highlights challenges related to real-time processing capabilities for instant diagnostics.
The authors note accessibility and usability issues in remote or resource-limited areas as a significant constraint for deployment.

The dataset is described as comprising 'acne and benign skin cancer images' but lacks specific details on sample sizes, class distribution, or source provenance, raising potential data leakage or bias concerns.
The paper reports two different accuracy figures (96.4% in the abstract and 97.858% in the results), which may indicate inconsistency in reporting or different evaluation splits not clearly defined.
The study is published in a conference proceeding (ICNSoC) rather than a peer-reviewed journal, which may affect the rigor of the validation process.
The model architecture details (e.g., specific layer counts, kernel sizes, optimizer settings) are not provided, limiting reproducibility.
No external validation or cross-dataset testing is reported, suggesting the results may be overfitted to the specific custom dataset used.
